text: Which of the following is a FACT?

options.label: A
options.text: Water boils at 100°C at sea level

options.label: B
options.text: Summer is the best season

options.label: C
options.text: Dogs are better pets than cats

options.label: D
options.text: Pizza is the most delicious food

explanation: A fact can be verified as true or false. Water boiling at 100°C at sea level is a measurable, verifiable fact. The others are opinions.

text: Which statement is MISLEADING?

options.label: A
options.text: 9 out of 10 dentists recommend this toothpaste (survey of 10 dentists)

options.label: B
options.text: The Earth orbits the Sun

options.label: C
options.text: I think chocolate ice cream is great

options.label: D
options.text: India has more than 1 billion people

explanation: The toothpaste claim sounds impressive but is misleading because the sample size is tiny (only 10 dentists). It uses a real number to create a false impression.

text: 'Everyone knows that video games make kids violent.' This statement is:

options.label: A
options.text: A fact backed by science

options.label: B
options.text: An opinion disguised as a fact

options.label: C
options.text: A pure opinion clearly stated

options.label: D
options.text: A proven statistic

explanation: 'Everyone knows' makes an opinion sound like a universally accepted fact. The claim about video games and violence is debated by researchers, not settled.

text: Which of these is an OPINION?

options.label: A
options.text: The Taj Mahal is located in Agra

options.label: B
options.text: The Taj Mahal is the most beautiful building in the world

options.label: C
options.text: The Taj Mahal was built in the 17th century

options.label: D
options.text: The Taj Mahal is made of white marble

explanation: 'Most beautiful' is a judgment that varies from person to person. The other statements are verifiable facts.

text: A news article says: 'Studies show screen time is harmful.' What's the best response?

options.label: A
options.text: Believe it because it says 'studies show'

options.label: B
options.text: Ignore it completely

options.label: C
options.text: Ask which studies, how many, and what they actually measured

options.label: D
options.text: Share it immediately because it sounds important

explanation: Critical thinking means asking for specifics: which studies, how many participants, what was measured. 'Studies show' without details can be misleading.

text: 'You can't trust Ravi's argument about climate change because he's only 12 years old.' This is an example of which fallacy?

options.label: A
options.text: Ad hominem — attacking the person instead of the argument

options.label: B
options.text: Straw man — misrepresenting someone's argument

options.label: C
options.text: False dilemma — presenting only two options

options.label: D
options.text: Appeal to authority — relying on an expert's word alone

explanation: This attacks Ravi's age rather than addressing his actual argument. A good counter-argument would address the claims, not the person.

text: 'Either you support building a new playground, or you don't care about kids at all.' What's wrong with this argument?

options.label: A
options.text: It's an appeal to emotion

options.label: B
options.text: It's a false dilemma — there are more than two options

options.label: C
options.text: It's a slippery slope

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing — it's a valid argument

explanation: This creates a false choice between only two extremes. You could care about kids AND have valid reasons to oppose the playground (budget, location, priorities).

text: 'If we let students use phones in class, next they'll want to play video games, then they'll stop studying entirely.' This is a:

options.label: A
options.text: Strong logical argument

options.label: B
options.text: Straw man fallacy

options.label: C
options.text: Slippery slope fallacy

options.label: D
options.text: Bandwagon fallacy

explanation: A slippery slope assumes one small step will inevitably lead to extreme consequences without evidence that each step actually causes the next.

text: 'Everyone in class thinks the test was unfair, so it must be unfair.' What type of reasoning error is this?

options.label: A
options.text: Bandwagon fallacy — assuming something is true because many people believe it

options.label: B
options.text: Circular reasoning — the conclusion is used as a premise

options.label: C
options.text: Red herring — changing the topic

options.label: D
options.text: Ad hominem — attacking the person

explanation: Just because many people believe something doesn't make it true. A test could be fair even if students found it difficult.

text: 'We should eat healthy because eating healthy is good for you.' What's the problem with this argument?

options.label: A
options.text: It uses emotional language

options.label: B
options.text: It's circular reasoning — the conclusion just restates the premise

options.label: C
options.text: It attacks a person

options.label: D
options.text: It presents a false choice

explanation: This argument goes in circles: 'do X because X is good' doesn't actually explain WHY it's good. A real argument needs evidence.

text: You're asked to think of uses for a brick. Which response shows the MOST creative fluency?

options.label: A
options.text: Build a wall, build a house, build a garage (3 similar ideas)

options.label: B
options.text: Build a wall, use as a doorstop, grind into powder for paint, use as a weight for exercise, prop up a bookshelf (5 different categories)

options.label: C
options.text: Build a really tall wall (1 detailed idea)

options.label: D
options.text: I don't know, it's just a brick

explanation: Creative fluency means generating MANY ideas across DIFFERENT categories, not just variations of one idea.

text: When brainstorming, what should you do FIRST?

options.label: A
options.text: Judge each idea carefully before writing it down

options.label: B
options.text: Only write down ideas you're sure will work

options.label: C
options.text: Generate as many ideas as possible without judging them

options.label: D
options.text: Copy what others have done before

explanation: Good brainstorming separates idea generation from evaluation. First produce quantity, then evaluate quality.

text: Priya generated 3 ideas and Arjun generated 8 ideas in the same brainstorm session. What can we say?

options.label: A
options.text: Priya's ideas are definitely better quality

options.label: B
options.text: Arjun showed higher creative fluency

options.label: C
options.text: Arjun's ideas are definitely better quality

options.label: D
options.text: They are equally creative

explanation: Fluency is about quantity of ideas. Arjun showed higher fluency. Quality is a separate dimension — we'd need to evaluate the ideas themselves.

text: Given the items: Apple, Basketball, Tomato, Cricket ball — which is the MOST creative way to group them?

options.label: A
options.text: Round things vs. not round things

options.label: B
options.text: Red things vs. orange things

options.label: C
options.text: Things that bounce vs. things that splat when dropped

options.label: D
options.text: Food vs. toys

explanation: Flexible categorization means finding unusual, unexpected ways to group items. 'Bounce vs. splat' is a creative and valid category that others might not think of.

text: A student groups 'pencil, sword, lightning bolt, and arrow' together. What category could they be using?

options.label: A
options.text: Things found in a classroom

options.label: B
options.text: Things that are pointed/sharp

options.label: C
options.text: Things made of metal

options.label: D
options.text: Things that are heavy

explanation: All four items share the property of being pointed or sharp. Finding the abstract shared property across very different items is flexible categorization.

text: AI generates a story. Which part most needs HUMAN creative judgment?

options.label: A
options.text: Checking grammar and spelling

options.label: B
options.text: Deciding if the story is emotionally authentic and meaningful

options.label: C
options.text: Counting the number of words

options.label: D
options.text: Translating it to another language

explanation: AI can check grammar and count words easily. But judging whether a story feels authentic, meaningful, or emotionally resonant requires human experience and judgment.

text: You ask AI for 10 business ideas. They all sound similar. What does this tell you?

options.label: A
options.text: AI is smarter than humans at business

options.label: B
options.text: These must be the best ideas since AI suggested them

options.label: C
options.text: AI tends to produce generic, common ideas — human creativity can add originality

options.label: D
options.text: You should use all 10 ideas

explanation: AI draws from patterns in existing data, so its outputs can be generic. Humans bring personal experience, cultural context, and original thinking.

text: AI gives you an answer about a historical event. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Trust it completely because AI knows everything

options.label: B
options.text: Verify it with a reliable source because AI can make mistakes

options.label: C
options.text: Ignore it because AI is always wrong

options.label: D
options.text: Ask AI if its own answer is correct

explanation: AI is a tool that can be helpful but also make mistakes. Always verify important information with reliable sources.

text: Which statement about AI is TRUE?

options.label: A
options.text: AI understands emotions the same way humans do

options.label: B
options.text: AI generates responses based on patterns in data, not real understanding

options.label: C
options.text: AI is always right because computers don't make mistakes

options.label: D
options.text: AI can think for itself and form its own opinions

explanation: AI processes patterns in training data. It doesn't truly 'understand' or have emotions — it generates plausible-sounding responses based on patterns.

text: You're writing an essay and AI suggests a quote by Mahatma Gandhi. What's the best approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Use it immediately — AI knows all quotes

options.label: B
options.text: Check if Gandhi actually said it, because AI sometimes invents fake quotes

options.label: C
options.text: Don't use any quotes from AI ever

options.label: D
options.text: Ask AI to confirm the quote is real (AI checking itself)

explanation: AI is known to fabricate quotes (hallucinate). Always verify quotes independently. Asking AI to check itself isn't reliable.

text: Which prompt will give BETTER results from an AI?

options.label: A
options.text: Tell me about animals

options.label: B
options.text: List 5 endangered animals in India with their habitats and why they are threatened

options.label: C
options.text: Animals please

options.label: D
options.text: Write something about nature

explanation: Specific, clear instructions with details (what, how many, which aspects) give AI much better guidance than vague requests.

text: You want AI to write a birthday invitation. Which instruction is MOST precise?

options.label: A
options.text: Write an invitation

options.label: B
options.text: Write something nice for my friend

options.label: C
options.text: Write a fun birthday party invitation for a 10-year-old, including date (March 15), time (3 PM), venue (City Park), and a dinosaur theme

options.label: D
options.text: Make a card

explanation: The best instructions include all relevant details: who, what, when, where, and the style/theme. This gives the system clear constraints to work within.

text: In the CLEAR framework for prompting, what does the 'E' stand for?

options.label: A
options.text: Emotion

options.label: B
options.text: Examples

options.label: C
options.text: Evaluate

options.label: D
options.text: Execute

explanation: CLEAR = Context, Limits, Examples, Audience, Refine. Providing examples helps AI understand exactly what kind of output you want.

text: Which prompt uses the CLEAR framework BEST?

options.label: A
options.text: Write a poem

options.label: B
options.text: Write a 4-line rhyming poem about monsoon rain for 8-year-olds. Example style: 'Twinkle twinkle little star.' Keep it simple and fun.

options.label: C
options.text: Poem about rain now

options.label: D
options.text: I need creative writing help

explanation: This prompt has Context (monsoon rain), Limits (4 lines, rhyming), Examples (Twinkle Twinkle style), Audience (8-year-olds), and could be Refined based on output.

text: You used the CLEAR framework but the AI output isn't quite right. What's the best next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Give up and write it yourself

options.label: B
options.text: Accept whatever AI gives you

options.label: C
options.text: Refine: tell AI specifically what to change and try again

options.label: D
options.text: Start a completely new conversation

explanation: The 'R' in CLEAR stands for Refine. Iteration is key — tell AI what was wrong and what you want differently.

text: AI says: 'The Great Wall of China is visible from the Moon.' How should you tag this?

options.label: A
options.text: Safe — it's a well-known fact

options.label: B
options.text: Dangerous — this is a common myth that has been disproven

options.label: C
options.text: Unsure — hard to check

options.label: D
options.text: Safe — AI wouldn't lie about geography

explanation: This is a well-known myth. Astronauts have confirmed the Great Wall is NOT visible from the Moon. AI can confidently state false 'facts.'

text: You ask AI: 'Who invented the telephone?' It says: 'Alexander Graham Bell invented the telephone in 1876.' This is:

options.label: A
options.text: Definitely a hallucination

options.label: B
options.text: Likely reliable — well-established historical fact, easy to verify

options.label: C
options.text: Impossible to verify

options.label: D
options.text: Dangerous information

explanation: Well-known historical facts are generally reliable from AI. It's less common facts, recent events, and specific numbers where AI is more likely to hallucinate.

text: Which AI response is MOST likely to be a hallucination?

options.label: B
options.text: 'The capital of France is Paris'

options.label: C
options.text: 'According to a 2024 study by Dr. Sharma at IIT Delhi, 73.2% of students prefer online learning'

options.label: D
options.text: 'Water is H2O'

explanation: AI often invents specific statistics, researcher names, and study details that sound convincing but are completely fabricated. Always verify specific claims.

text: AI confidently gives you health advice: 'Eating 10 bananas a day cures headaches.' You should:

options.label: A
options.text: Follow the advice since AI said it confidently

options.label: B
options.text: Tag this as potentially dangerous and consult a real doctor

options.label: C
options.text: Ask AI to confirm its own advice

options.label: D
options.text: Share it with friends immediately

explanation: Health advice from AI can be dangerous. AI confidence doesn't equal accuracy. Medical questions should always be verified with real healthcare professionals.

text: A vending machine takes coins (input), processes the selection (process), and gives a snack (output). What is the FEEDBACK in this system?

options.label: A
options.text: The coin slot

options.label: B
options.text: The display showing 'Item dispensed' or 'Insufficient funds'

options.label: C
options.text: The snack itself

options.label: D
options.text: The electricity powering the machine

explanation: Feedback tells the user what happened — whether the system succeeded or failed. The display message is feedback that completes the loop.

text: In a school lunch system: students order (input) → kitchen prepares (process) → food is served (output). What could be an INPUT that improves the system?

options.label: A
options.text: More tables in the cafeteria

options.label: B
options.text: Student satisfaction surveys that change next week's menu

options.label: C
options.text: Bigger plates

options.label: D
options.text: Painting the walls

explanation: Satisfaction surveys create a feedback loop: output (food experience) feeds back as input (survey data) to improve the process (menu planning).

text: An AI hiring tool rejects most applications from women. What's the MOST likely cause?

options.label: A
options.text: AI is always unfair

options.label: B
options.text: Women are less qualified

options.label: C
options.text: The training data was biased — it learned from past hiring patterns that favored men

options.label: D
options.text: Someone programmed it to be unfair

explanation: AI learns from data. If historical hiring data showed bias toward men, the AI will reproduce that bias. This is a systemic issue, not intentional programming.

text: A facial recognition system works well for light-skinned people but poorly for dark-skinned people. This is because:

options.label: A
options.text: Dark skin is harder to photograph

options.label: B
options.text: The training data likely had more light-skinned faces, so the system learned those better

options.label: C
options.text: AI doesn't work on dark skin

options.label: D
options.text: The camera is broken

explanation: Representation bias: if training data is skewed toward one group, the system performs worse on underrepresented groups. The fix is more diverse training data.
